review_candidates.comment: The events establish that the wife was brought to the grove where the husband was bound (E_bring_wife) and that intercourse occurred (E_intercourse) with husband as a knowing observer. Characterizing this as coercion is well-grounded in those structural facts. The additional texture — Tajomaru pleading afterward — is plausible woodcutter-testimony interpretation that does not contradict any event in view.

The claim that the intercourse event is the narrative pivot and that each testimony's texture is a self-portrait is a reasonable structural observation. The one branch-specific texture visible in this view (D_intercourse_woodcutter_texture) supports the frame: the woodcutter's emphasis on coercion and Tajomaru's pleading positions the woodcutter as a dispassionate outside observer, which is itself a self-characterization. I cannot verify the claim for all four testimonies from this view alone, but nothing here contradicts it.

E_wc_wife_goads is an utterance by the wife directed at both tajomaru and husband. The motivation description — that she shames both men into fighting — is consistent with the subsequent combat event (E_wc_fight) and with D_wc_fight_character's depiction of the fight as reluctant and clumsy. The interpretation coherently links the utterance to the combat that follows.

The texture of a cowardly, clumsy skirmish is a coherent interpretation of the woodcutter's testimony. E_wc_fight confirms combat occurred and that Tajomaru killed the husband with a sword. The description's meta-observation — that the woodcutter's version denies both men dignity — aligns with the broader pattern in this branch where the woodcutter positions himself as the only clear-eyed observer of degraded participants.

E_wc_theft confirms the woodcutter stole the dagger, grounding the 'self-incrimination reads as candor' claim. The balancing observation — that he is the last witness with his own concealment to protect — is supported by the gap between E_body_found (τ_s 30, his official story) and his actual presence during E_wc_fight (τ_s 9). The stated agnosticism ('this encoding does the same') is an appropriate authorial posture for a substrate modeling contested accounts.

This is a well-formed authorial uncertainty. It identifies a genuine interpretive crux — the film's degree of endorsement of the woodcutter — and correctly locates D_woodcutter_trust as the place where a revised stance would surface. The self-awareness about encoding fidelity is exactly what authorial-uncertainty descriptions should express.

proposed_description.text: The current representation in D_woodcutter_trust is well-calibrated for this encoding's purposes. Within the view, the woodcutter's account both self-incriminates (E_wc_theft: he stole the dagger) and self-serves (he degrades every other participant — the fight is clumsy per D_wc_fight_character, the wife is the instigator per D_wc_wife_goads — while casting himself as a mere observer). This double signal is precisely what makes him the story's hardest trust case. The structural encoding already mirrors the film's ambiguity: his testimony lives on :b-woodcutter as one contested branch, not promoted to :canonical, while the theft event gives future readers a concrete reason to interrogate his reliability. Unless a later pass surfaces evidence that the film privileges his account over the others — e.g., through framing devices or narrative sequencing that the current view does not encode — the agnostic stance should hold.

answer_proposals.rationale: The events on :b-woodcutter show both the woodcutter's self-incrimination (E_wc_theft) and his narrative self-positioning (degrading the combatants, blaming the wife), which together support maintaining the balanced trust-flag rather than tilting toward endorsement.
